practices.title: Never hardcode secrets in source code
practices.explanation: Hardcoded secrets are committed to version control and exposed in class files. Use environment variables or a vault.

practices.title: Use PreparedStatement — never concatenate SQL strings
practices.bad: stmt.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + userId);
practices.good:
PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");
ps.setString(1, userId);
practices.explanation: String concatenation in SQL enables injection attacks. PreparedStatement escapes inputs automatically.

practices.title: Validate and sanitize all external input
practices.explanation: Unvalidated input can lead to injection attacks and data corruption. Validate format and range at entry points.

practices.title: Never use Runtime.exec with unsanitized input
practices.good:
ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder("cmd", "/c", sanitizedCmd);
pb.start();
practices.explanation: Constructing shell commands from user input enables command injection. Use ProcessBuilder with argument arrays.

practices.title: Keep methods under 50 lines
practices.explanation: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and modify. Extract logical steps into named methods.

practices.title: Avoid God classes — keep classes under 300 lines
practices.bad: // 500-line class mixing data access, business logic, and validation
practices.explanation: Large classes violate Single Responsibility Principle and are hard to test. Split by concern.

practices.title: Use enums instead of string/int constants
practices.bad:
public static final int STATUS_ACTIVE = 1;
public static final int STATUS_INACTIVE = 2;
practices.explanation: Magic constants lack type safety and allow invalid values. Enums provide compile-time checking and self-documentation.

practices.title: Prefer composition and interfaces over deep inheritance
practices.bad: class PremiumGoldInternationalUser extends PremiumGoldUser extends PremiumUser extends User { }
practices.explanation: Deep inheritance hierarchies are rigid and hard to modify. Prefer composition with interfaces for flexible behaviour.

practices.title: Use try-with-resources for AutoCloseable objects
practices.explanation: Manual close() is skipped on exceptions, leaking resources. try-with-resources guarantees cleanup.

practices.title: Catch specific exceptions — never catch generic Exception
practices.good: try { op(); } catch (IOException e) { handleIO(e); } catch (ParseException e) { handleParse(e); }
practices.explanation: Catching Exception hides programming errors (NPE, IllegalArgument) that should propagate. Catch only expected checked exceptions.

practices.title: Check for null before dereferencing
practices.bad:
User user = repository.findById(id);
String name = user.getName();
practices.explanation: Null pointer dereference is the most common runtime exception. Guard nullable returns with explicit checks or Optional.

practices.title: Handle InterruptedException correctly — don't swallow it
practices.explanation: Swallowing InterruptedException breaks thread cancellation. Re-set the interrupt flag and propagate.

practices.title: Use StringBuilder in loops — not string concatenation
practices.explanation: String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) intermediate objects. StringBuilder appends in-place.

practices.title: Avoid unnecessary autoboxing — use primitives where possible
practices.explanation: Autoboxing creates unnecessary Integer objects on every operation. Use primitive types in hot paths.

practices.title: Use connection pooling — never create connections per request
practices.explanation: Creating database connections is expensive. Use a pool (HikariCP, c3p0) to reuse connections.

practices.title: Avoid N+1 queries — batch database access
practices.good:
List<Long> ids = users.stream().map(User::getId).toList();
Map<Long, List<Order>> orders = orderRepo.findByUserIds(ids);
practices.explanation: Querying inside a loop creates N+1 round-trips. Batch into a single query with IN clause.
